worth_it_explanation: The measured rate, meaning the share of a campaign's impressions that DoubleVerify actually assessed for viewability, fraud, and brand safety, determines how much of the quality data in DV Pinnacle reflects the real media buy. Impressions DV did not measure are absent from every quality report: no viewability score, no fraud detection, no brand safety classification. A low or declining measured rate does not mean the campaign is performing well in those unmeasured placements. It means there is no data. These checks confirm that the measured rate is being monitored, that the causes of coverage gaps are diagnosed rather than ignored, and that the impression count DV reports as measured is compared against what the demand-side platform (DSP) or ad server billed. All three are configuration and workflow changes, not rebuilds.
intro:
DoubleVerify (DV) measures ad impressions by deploying a JavaScript tag or a server-to-server integration that lets DV assess each impression for viewability, invalid traffic (IVT), and brand suitability. The measured rate is the share of total monitored impressions where DV was able to complete that assessment. Impressions that DV monitors but cannot measure, due to tag deployment gaps, environments that block JavaScript execution, or buying platforms without a complete DV integration, receive no quality data in DV Pinnacle.

The distinction matters because viewability rates, fraud rates, and brand safety rates reported in Pinnacle are calculated against measured impressions, not total impressions. When the measured rate is low, those quality metrics describe only the subset DV reached, not the full campaign. A campaign with a 60% measured rate has no quality data for 40% of its impressions. If the unmeasured portion runs in lower-quality placements, the blended quality performance is worse than what Pinnacle shows.

These checks assess whether the measured rate is tracked at a campaign level, whether coverage gaps are diagnosed when they appear, and whether the impression count DV reports is reconciled against the count the buying platform billed.

audit_checks.title: The measured rate is not tracked in Pinnacle, so the team does not know what share of spend has quality data
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the measured rate is not tracked, every quality metric DV reports, including viewability rate, fraud rate, and brand safety rate, is calculated against an unknown subset of impressions with no indication of how large that subset is. The team cannot assess whether the quality data in Pinnacle reflects 95% of the campaign or 50% of it. A high viewability rate means something different at each of those coverage levels, and the difference is invisible without the measured rate alongside it.

educate.base:
DV Pinnacle distinguishes between monitored impressions and measured impressions. Monitored impressions are the total count of ad impressions DV has visibility into across active campaigns. Measured impressions are the subset where DV's tag or integration successfully executed and produced quality data: a viewability assessment, an invalid traffic (IVT) determination, or a brand safety classification. The measured rate is the ratio of measured impressions to monitored impressions for a given campaign, channel, or time period.

When DV reports that a campaign achieved a 72% viewability rate, that figure is calculated against measured impressions only. If the measured rate for that campaign is 55%, the 72% viewability rate describes just over half the impressions served. The other 45% have no viewability data in Pinnacle. Whether those impressions were more or less viewable than the measured half is unknown.

The practical consequence is that quality benchmarks drawn from Pinnacle data carry an unstated caveat about coverage. A campaign that appears to meet viewability targets may be meeting them on the measured portion while the unmeasured portion includes a disproportionate share of below-the-fold placements or environments DV cannot reach. The team cannot know this without the measured rate as a parallel metric.

DV Pinnacle includes measured impressions and monitored ads as separate report columns. A few signs the measured rate is not being tracked:
  • Standard campaign reports in Pinnacle show viewability rate, fraud rate, and brand safety rate but do not include a measured impressions column alongside monitored impressions
  • There is no documented baseline for what measured rate this campaign or channel typically achieves
  • Quality metrics are discussed in reporting without reference to what share of impressions they are based on
You may want to confirm that measured impressions and monitored impressions are both present in the report template used for each active campaign before drawing conclusions from the quality metrics.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running display and video campaigns across multiple DSPs and social platforms, measured rate will differ by channel: open web display typically achieves higher coverage than in-app or social environments where JavaScript restrictions reduce DV's access. You may want to confirm that Pinnacle reports show measured rate by channel so quality benchmarks can be read in context.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser running prospecting campaigns across programmatic and social inventory, quality targets set for the campaign should be interpreted alongside the measured rate for each channel. You may want to confirm that Pinnacle campaign reports include the measured impressions column so that a strong viewability rate is not mistaken for a signal that covers the full buy.

learn_more.source_title: MRC standards and guidelines index, including viewable ad impression measurement standards

educate.learn_more_note: Guidance on how DV defines and reports measured rate and monitored impressions, and how to add these columns to a Pinnacle delivery report, is available in the DoubleVerify Pinnacle knowledge base, which requires a Pinnacle login. Contact your DoubleVerify account team for reporting configuration documentation.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether the standard campaign report in Pinnacle includes both measured impressions and monitored impressions, and whether the measured rate is reviewed alongside quality metrics.

dv_pinnacle.label: DoubleVerify Pinnacle
steps: Log in to DV Pinnacle and open the Delivery report for the campaign or advertiser you are reviewing.

In the Metrics section of the report configuration, confirm whether 'Measured Impressions' and 'Monitored Ads' (or equivalent columns) are included. If they are not present, note that the current report template does not expose coverage data.
If both columns are present, calculate the measured rate as: measured impressions divided by monitored impressions. Note the result for each campaign or channel in scope.
Compare the measured rate against the campaign's quality metrics, such as viewability rate or fraud rate, to assess how much of the buy those quality figures are based on.

Check whether a documented baseline exists for what measured rate this campaign, channel, or DSP integration typically achieves. If no baseline exists, the current figure has no reference point for comparison.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether measured impressions and monitored impressions are both present in the standard Pinnacle campaign report, and whether the measured rate is reviewed alongside viewability, fraud, and brand safety metrics. If both columns are present and the measured rate is documented alongside quality benchmarks, the check passes. If quality metrics are reported without a corresponding measured rate, the check is a fail recorded with the campaign name, the quality metrics currently shown, and the missing coverage column.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether measured impressions and monitored impressions are both present in the Pinnacle delivery report, and note the calculated measured rate for each campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s standard campaign reporting in DV Pinnacle does not include the measured rate alongside quality metrics such as viewability rate and fraud rate. DV reports viewability, invalid traffic, and brand safety figures against the impressions it was able to measure, not against the full impression volume. Without the measured rate as a parallel metric, there is no way to assess what share of the campaign's spend those quality figures are based on. {{voice_recommend}} adding measured impressions and monitored impressions as standard columns in the Pinnacle delivery report template and reviewing the measured rate alongside quality benchmarks for each active campaign.

audit_checks.title: A low or declining measured rate in Pinnacle is not investigated against its causes
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A low measured rate is a symptom with several possible causes, including a missing or misfiring DV tag, a buying platform integration that has degraded, or a shift in spend toward environments where DV cannot execute its JavaScript. Without a diagnostic process to distinguish among these causes, the team cannot determine whether the gap is a configuration error that can be corrected or an environmental constraint that requires a different measurement approach.

educate.base:
When the measured rate for a campaign or channel is lower than expected, the cause typically falls into one of a few categories. A DV tag that was not placed on a creative, or that was placed incorrectly, means that impressions from that creative contribute to monitored count but cannot be measured. A demand-side platform (DSP) integration between DV and the buying platform may have been misconfigured or may not support all ad formats or environments the campaign uses. Some media environments, such as mobile in-app inventory where JavaScript execution is restricted, achieve structurally lower measured rates than open web display. A shift in campaign spend toward in-app, connected TV (CTV), or certain social inventory can reduce the measured rate even when the DV deployment itself has not changed.

The Media Rating Council (MRC) accredits third-party measurement vendors, including DV, against defined methodologies for impression measurement. Those accreditations apply to impressions DV is able to reach and assess. Environments that prevent DV's measurement mechanism from executing fall outside the accredited measurement scope for that campaign. The measured rate tells the practitioner what share of the campaign falls within that accredited scope.

Distinguishing a correctable configuration gap from an environmental constraint matters because the remedies are different. A missing tag is fixed in the ad server or creative trafficking workflow. A DSP integration issue is resolved by reviewing the integration settings in Pinnacle and with the DSP's technical team. A structural in-app or CTV coverage limit may require a different measurement path, such as Open Measurement Software Development Kit (OM SDK) integration for mobile in-app environments.

A few signs a low measured rate has not been investigated:
  • The measured rate for a campaign or channel is below the account's typical baseline and there is no explanation on file for the difference
  • The measured rate has declined over multiple reporting periods without a documented inquiry into whether the tag deployment or integration changed
  • The team attributes a low measured rate entirely to in-app inventory without confirming whether tag deployment on web and CTV inventory in the same campaign is intact
You may want to confirm that a diagnostic process is in place for any campaign or channel where the measured rate falls materially below the account baseline, before that coverage gap is carried forward into a new flight.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running campaigns that include in-app display alongside open web, the in-app portion will structurally achieve a lower measured rate due to JavaScript restrictions common in app environments. You may want to confirm that the low in-app measured rate reflects that environmental constraint rather than a missing tag, and that the open web and CTV portions of the same campaign have expected coverage.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser whose campaigns include a mix of programmatic display and CTV, a declining measured rate on CTV may reflect a change in DV's integration with the buying platform rather than an environmental constraint. You may want to confirm with the DSP's technical team whether the DV integration is active and correctly configured for the CTV inventory being purchased.

learn_more.source_title: MRC standards and guidelines index, including viewable ad impression measurement and invalid traffic detection guidelines
Open Measurement SDK: cross-environment impression measurement standard for mobile, video, and CTV

educate.learn_more_note: Guidance on diagnosing measured rate issues by ad format and buying platform integration in DV Pinnacle is available in the DoubleVerify Pinnacle knowledge base, which requires a Pinnacle login. Contact your DoubleVerify account team or technical implementation specialist for integration-specific documentation.

investigate.intro: Review the measured rate by channel and creative type to identify where coverage is lowest, then assess whether the gap reflects a configuration issue or an environmental constraint.

dv_pinnacle.label: DoubleVerify Pinnacle
steps: In DV Pinnacle, open a Delivery report segmented by channel or buying platform, with measured impressions and monitored impressions both included. Calculate the measured rate for each segment: measured impressions divided by monitored impressions.

Identify any channel or buying platform where the measured rate is materially lower than the account baseline. Note whether the low-coverage segment is in-app, CTV, social, or open web programmatic, as each environment has a different structural ceiling for DV measurement.

For channels where coverage is below the account baseline, check whether the DV integration with the relevant DSP or buying platform is listed as active in Pinnacle's account settings or integration panel.

For campaigns running display creatives, confirm in the ad server or DSP that the DV tag is present and correctly formatted on each active creative. A missing or malformed tag will show monitored impressions without corresponding measured impressions.

Check whether the campaign's media mix has shifted toward in-app, CTV, or other lower-coverage environments since the last reporting period. A mix shift can reduce the measured rate without any change to the tag deployment.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the measured rate by channel or buying platform and identifies any segment where coverage is materially below the account baseline. For each low-coverage segment, the practitioner documents whether the cause is a correctable configuration gap, such as a missing tag or a degraded DSP integration, or an environmental constraint, such as in-app JavaScript restrictions. A campaign where the measured rate is within the expected range by channel, or where any gap is documented with a known cause, passes. A campaign where a low measured rate has no documented explanation and no diagnostic steps have been taken is a fail, recorded with the segment name and the measured rate observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the measured rate by channel alongside the account baseline and note any diagnosed causes for the gaps above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s DV Pinnacle measured rate for one or more campaigns or channels is below the account baseline, and that no documented diagnostic process exists to determine the cause. A low measured rate may reflect a missing tag, a degraded DSP integration, or a shift in spend toward environments with structural JavaScript measurement constraints. Without distinguishing among these causes, the coverage gap cannot be resolved. {{voice_recommend}} segmenting the Pinnacle delivery report by channel and buying platform to identify where measured rate falls below baseline, confirming that the DV tag is present and correctly formatted on each active creative in the affected segments, and reviewing the DV integration settings for any DSP where coverage has declined.

audit_checks.title: The measured impression count in Pinnacle is not reconciled against the billed impression count from the DSP or ad server
audit_checks.impact_rationale: DV's measured impression count and the DSP's billed impression count are derived from separate systems with different methodologies. A reconciliation step confirms whether the gap between the two is within an expected methodological range or indicates a configuration problem. Without reconciliation, a large coverage shortfall, one that would change how the team reads the quality data, can go unnoticed until a campaign has finished.

educate.base:
The impression count a DSP or ad server bills against and the impression count DV reports as measured will not match exactly. Some gap is expected. The DSP counts an impression at the time it sends the ad call. DV counts a measured impression when its tag or integration successfully executes. Differences in counting windows, server-to-server versus browser-based measurement, ad blocking, and the latency at which each system records a transaction all contribute to a gap that practitioners typically accept as methodological.

What is not expected is a gap that grows substantially week over week, that differs significantly by buying platform without a documented reason, or that is large enough to call into question whether DV's quality data reflects a representative sample of what the DSP billed. A campaign where DV reports 6 million measured impressions and the DSP billed 10 million impressions has a 40% coverage gap. That gap means 40% of billed impressions have no viewability, fraud, or brand safety data in Pinnacle. Whether that share is large enough to affect conclusions depends on the campaign, but the team needs to know it exists.

Reconciliation means pulling the measured impression total from DV Pinnacle and the billed impression total from the DSP or ad server for the same campaign and date range, calculating the gap as a percentage, and comparing it against a documented baseline or an expected range. The MRC's guidelines for ad impression measurement describe the methodological sources of expected discrepancy between counting systems. Where DV's count is substantially lower than the DSP's billed count, the difference is worth tracing before the campaign ends, not after.

A few signs reconciliation is not being performed:
  • Post-campaign or mid-flight reports reference DV quality metrics without noting whether the measured impression count aligns with the DSP's delivery report for the same period
  • There is no documented expectation for what gap between DV measured impressions and DSP billed impressions is considered within range for this account or channel
  • The team has discovered a large discrepancy between DV and DSP impression counts only at the end of a campaign, with no mid-flight review on file
You may want to establish a reconciliation baseline, meaning the expected discrepancy range between DV measured impressions and DSP billed impressions for each buying platform, before the next campaign flight begins.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running campaigns across multiple DSPs, the measured-to-billed impression gap may differ by platform. You may want to confirm that a reconciliation baseline exists for each active DSP integration so a growing gap on one platform can be identified without waiting for the post-campaign report.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser running a programmatic campaign with a defined viewability threshold as part of a direct deal, confirming that DV's measured impression count is close to the DSP's billed count is part of verifying that the measurement data covers the inventory the deal guaranteed. You may want to confirm a reconciliation step is included in the campaign setup checklist.

learn_more.source_title: MRC standards and guidelines index, including desktop display and digital video impression measurement guidelines

educate.learn_more_note: Guidance on how DV's measured impression count compares to billed impressions by buying platform, and how to configure Pinnacle reports for reconciliation, is available in the DoubleVerify Pinnacle knowledge base, which requires a Pinnacle login. Contact your DoubleVerify account team for reconciliation documentation and expected discrepancy ranges by integration type.

investigate.intro: Pull the measured impression total from Pinnacle and the billed impression total from the DSP or ad server for the same campaign and date range, then calculate and document the gap.

dv_pinnacle.label: DoubleVerify Pinnacle
steps: In DV Pinnacle, open a Delivery report for the campaign in scope. Set the date range to match the period you want to reconcile. Add the 'Measured Impressions' column to the report and export the total.
In the DSP or ad server, pull a delivery report for the same campaign and the same date range. Note the total billed impressions.
Calculate the gap: (DSP billed impressions minus DV measured impressions) divided by DSP billed impressions. Express this as a percentage.
Compare the gap against any documented baseline for this buying platform. If no baseline exists, note the gap percentage and flag it as the starting reference point for future reconciliation.

If the gap is larger than expected, or if no prior reconciliation has been performed, open the Pinnacle integration settings for the relevant DSP and confirm the integration is listed as active and correctly configured for the ad formats in this campaign.

Note whether the gap varies by creative format, such as display versus video, or by environment, such as web versus in-app, as methodological differences by format are a documented source of measurement discrepancy.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the gap between DV measured impressions and DSP billed impressions as a percentage, and compares it against a documented baseline or expected range for this buying platform. A campaign where the gap is within the documented expected range, or where the gap is documented for the first time as a new baseline, passes. A campaign where the gap substantially exceeds any prior baseline without a documented explanation, or where no reconciliation has been performed, is a fail recorded with the campaign name, the DV measured impression total, the DSP billed impression total, and the calculated gap percentage.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the DV measured impression total, the DSP billed impression total, and the calculated gap percentage for the campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s DV Pinnacle measured impression count is not reconciled against the billed impression count from the demand-side platform (DSP) or ad server at any point during or after campaign flights. Some gap between DV's measured count and the DSP's billed count is expected due to methodological differences in how each system records an impression. Without a documented baseline and a regular reconciliation step, a coverage gap large enough to change how the quality data reads can go unnoticed until a campaign has finished. {{voice_recommend}} establishing a reconciliation baseline for each active DSP integration by pulling DV measured impressions and DSP billed impressions for the same date range, calculating the gap as a percentage, and reviewing that figure at a consistent cadence during future flights.
